claims.text: Edgar Cayce, nicknamed 'the sleeping prophet,' made predictions about world events while in self-induced hypnotic trances between the 1900s and 1940s.

evidence.quote:
edgar cayce has
been called the sleeping prophet

so casey
kicked off his shoes laid back and put
himself into a deep trance

claims.rationale: Cayce's life, trance practice, and public nickname are historical fact. Born 1877, died 1945; gave thousands of trance readings — well-attested in biographies and the A.R.E. archives.

claims.text: At age 11 Cayce learned to memorize his schoolbooks overnight simply by sleeping on them.

evidence.quote:
when he woke up edgar knew everything in
the book like everything he was able to
recite the book word for word so edgar
began studying by sleeping on his books

claims.rationale: This is a standard Cayce hagiography anecdote from his own accounts/family testimony. Extraordinary memory-via-sleep has no mechanism in cognitive science; likely embellished or recast family folklore.

claims.text: In January 1934 Cayce predicted Hitler's rise to power and the outbreak of World War II, including the Germany-Austria-Japan alliance and US entry in 1941.

evidence.quote:
in january 1934 casey
predicted that hitler would rise in
power and reign over germany

in 1935 casey predicted the
outbreak of world war ii he saw an
alliance between austria germany and
japan

claims.rationale: That Cayce made forward-looking political statements is documented in the A.R.E. transcripts, but after-the-fact curation, ambiguous wording, and the Barnum effect (which c_0010 flags) weaken the prediction's evidentiary force. Hitler was already a rising figure in 1934.

claims.text: Cayce's 1938 prediction that the sunken ruins of Atlantis would rise near Bimini in 1968 came true with the discovery of the Bimini Road.

evidence.quote:
in 1938 casey said a
portion of temples may yet be discovered
under the sea near what is known as
bimini off the coast of florida expect
the rising of atlantis in 68 and sure
enough 30 years later in 1968 the bimini
road was discovered in the bahamas

claims.rationale: The Bimini Road is a documented underwater rock feature, but geological consensus identifies it as a naturally fractured beachrock formation, not an Atlantean temple. The 'prediction met' framing relies on loose pattern-matching — see c_0010.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] General skepticism about Cayce's hit-rate applies specifically to this Bimini 'match.'

claims.text: Cayce predicted Russia would eventually abandon communism and become free — a forecast borne out by the Soviet Union's 1991 dissolution.

evidence.quote:
casey said from russia
comes the hope of the world not in
respect to communism but freedom it will
take years to crystallize but russia
will once again be free

in 1991 the
soviet union dissolved and abandoned
communism

claims.rationale: Soviet dissolution in 1991 is historical fact. Whether Cayce's vague quote constitutes a real prediction depends heavily on interpretation; 'freedom from communism' was a standard Cold-War Western expectation, not a unique paranormal insight. directTruth kept moderate rather than low because the exact quote on record and the 1991 outcome do match.

dependencies.rationale: Another data point in Cayce's trance-reading output, supporting the basic historical premise of his career.

claims.text: Cayce described the Essene Jewish sect accurately in 1934, anticipating the documentary record later revealed by the 1947 Dead Sea Scrolls.

evidence.quote:
in 1934 not much was known
about the jewish sect called the essenes
but casey described the sect in detail
and his descriptions were determined to
be accurate

12
years later the dead sea scrolls written
by the essenes were discovered

claims.rationale: Essenes were actually known in pre-1934 scholarship (Josephus, Pliny, Philo all describe them). The 'nothing was known' framing is overstated. Dead Sea Scrolls were found 1947. Whether Cayce's descriptions were specifically validated by the scrolls is contested.

claims.text: Cayce predicted that a global pole shift of 16-20 degrees will occur once Mount Etna and Mount Pelée erupt simultaneously, giving humanity 90 days to evacuate the coasts.

evidence.quote:
in 1936 edgar cayce predicted that the
world would undergo a pole shift between
16 and 20 degrees and this would happen
when both mount etna and italy and mount
pele and martinique erupt together when
that happens we'll have 90 days to
evacuate the coastlines

claims.rationale: A 16-20 degree crustal pole shift on a 90-day timescale is geophysically impossible — the lithosphere cannot migrate that fast without energies that would vaporize the biosphere. True geographic/magnetic pole wander is measured in centimeters to meters per year.

claims.text: Once the predicted pole shift occurs, most of Northern Europe will sink, Japan will be submerged, and billions of people will be killed or displaced worldwide.

evidence.quote:
most of northern europe will simply be
gone sunk into the sea as the tectonic
plate underneath it collapses

billions of people will be displaced by
this cataclysm billions more will be
killed

claims.rationale: Dependent on c_0007, which is geophysically impossible. The specific continental map changes (Japan sinking, Northern Europe submerging) are Cayce's channelled imagery, not extrapolations from any physical model.

dependencies.rationale: Cannot be true unless the underlying pole shift occurs as Cayce described.

claims.text: Cayce predicted the pole shift would begin in 1998, and NASA subsequently detected a measurable pole shift starting in 1998.

evidence.quote:
when is this pole shift supposed to
happen well casey said 1998

nasa has detected a slight pole shift
that could be a concern when did they
detect the shift 1998.

claims.rationale: NASA has measured a small drift in Earth's rotational axis (several cm/year, increased after 2000 due to ice-mass loss) — but this is not a 16-20° catastrophic shift, just ordinary true polar wander. Conflating the two is a classic pseudo-science equivocation.

dependencies.rationale: Framed as confirming evidence of Cayce's pole-shift prediction, though the supporting data is a category error.

claims.text: Cayce's reputation as an accurate psychic relies on selection bias — his hits get remembered while his many misses are ignored, and his wording exhibits the vague Barnum-effect phrasing common to 'strip mall psychics'.

evidence.quote:
he gave over 14 000 readings
so he's going

casey used expressions like perhaps and
maybe and i feel that rather than using
positive declarations this is a common
technique used by strip mall psychics
this is known as the barnum effect

claims.rationale: This is the mainstream skeptical explanation for Cayce's track record, consistent with cognitive-psychology literature on the Barnum effect and confirmation bias. Host steelmans this position explicitly.

claims.text: Cayce did reading-based 'diagnoses' for people who were already dead at the time of the reading, with supporters excusing these failures as 'they would have lived if they'd applied the cure.'

evidence.quote:
casey
would receive a letter from a sick
person and then do a reading for them
giving them a diagnosis
casey didn't know that when he was in
his trance the people were already dead

claims.rationale: This specific failure mode and its ad-hoc defence are documented in Cayce-critical literature. Host uses it to undermine the paranormal-communication thesis.

dependencies.rationale: Concrete illustration of the selection-bias / hit-or-miss critique.
[undercuts] If Cayce could not detect that a petitioner was already dead, his trance state was not tapping genuine supernatural knowledge.

claims.text: Survey data show roughly 50% of American adults believe in some form of ESP or psychic phenomena, with believers tending toward emotional/creative thinking and lower stress levels than skeptics.

evidence.quote:
it found that 50 of
people believe in some form of esp or
psychic phenomena

those
quote-unquote gullible believers were
far happier in their lives and had much
lower levels of stress than skeptics

claims.rationale: Gallup and Pew polls have reported ~41-57% US belief in psychic phenomena depending on year/wording, so the ~50% figure is plausible. Cognitive-style / well-being correlations are less robust and depend heavily on measurement choices.

claims.text: Cayce never took payment for his readings, which distinguishes him from the class of frauds who exploit the bereaved for money.

evidence.quote:
edgar cayce never took a
penny he really wanted to help people

claims.rationale: Host's framing: Cayce took no personal fees (broadly accepted — his work was underwritten by the A.R.E. and donors). Whether this exempts him from the 'psychic frauds' category is a value judgment.
